items.title: Diagnosis of Systemic Amyloidosis: Utilizing Abdominal Fat Biopsy
items.content: For diagnosing systemic amyloidosis, an abdominal fat biopsy is a minimally invasive method that can be positive in more than 80% of patients. This technique involves aspirating subcutaneous fat from the abdominal wall using a 16-gauge needle after local anesthesia.
keyPoints: Abdominal fat biopsy is positive in >80% of systemic amyloidosis cases.
Fat globules are stained with Congo red dye to identify amyloid deposits.
This method avoids more invasive surgical procedures.

items.title: Identifying Amyloid Type: Mass Spectrometry's Role
items.content: Mass spectrometry-based analysis of amyloid-containing tissues is the preferred method for identifying the type of amyloid protein, offering high sensitivity and specificity. It surpasses immunochemical techniques by not requiring a large panel of antisera.
keyPoints: Sensitivity of mass spectrometry: 88%
Specificity of mass spectrometry: 96%
Mass spectrometry is superior to immunochemical methods for non-AL amyloidosis identification.

items.title: Characterization of Amyloid Deposits
items.content: Amyloid deposits exhibit a unique green birefringence when stained with Congo red dye and viewed under polarized light microscopy. This property, along with the ability to visualize 10-nm-diameter fibrils via electron microscopy, aids in their identification.
keyPoints: Green birefringence of amyloid deposits under polarized light after Congo red staining.
Amyloid fibrils are 10 nm in diameter and visualizable by electron microscopy.

items.title: Mechanisms and Toxicity of Amyloid Fibril Formation
items.content: The amyloid hypothesis suggests that precursor proteins misfold, forming oligomeric aggregates which are potentially more toxic than fibrils. These oligomers can interact with cells to induce reactive oxygen species and stress signaling, leading to organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Oligomeric intermediates may be the most toxic species in amyloidosis.
Direct proteotoxicity of soluble oligomers contributes to organ dysfunction.

items.title: Clinical Syndromes and Biomarkers in Amyloidosis
items.content: Amyloidosis presents with nonspecific laboratory alterations. Key biomarkers include elevated BNP or NT-proBNP for amyloid cardiomyopathy, which can be used for disease staging and monitoring. Renal insufficiency may falsely elevate these levels.
keyPoints: Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate is common.
Proteinuria in nephrotic range with hypoalbuminemia (<2 g/dL) often leads to pedal edema or anasarca.
BNP or NT-proBNP elevation indicates amyloid cardiomyopathy; renal insufficiency can falsely elevate these levels.

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of AL Amyloidosis
items.content: AL amyloidosis is most common in North America with an estimated incidence of 8–12 cases per 100,000 population. It typically occurs after age 40.
keyPoints: Most common type of systemic amyloidosis diagnosed in North America.
Incidence: 8–12 cases per 100,000 population.
Usually occurs after age 40.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Organ Involvement in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: AL amyloidosis often presents with nonspecific symptoms like fatigue and weight loss, but specific organ involvement is key for diagnosis. The kidneys are affected in 60–70% of patients, presenting as proteinuria, while cardiac involvement occurs in 70–80% of cases and is the leading cause of death.
keyPoints: Nonspecific symptoms: fatigue and weight loss.
Kidney involvement: 60–70% of patients; presents with proteinuria.
Cardiac involvement: 70–80% of patients; leading cause of death.

items.title: Diagnostic Features and Workup for AL Amyloidosis
items.content: Diagnosis involves identifying an underlying clonal plasma cell or B lymphoproliferative process. Key diagnostic features include renal proteinuria, cardiac abnormalities on ECG and echocardiogram, and multisystemic symptoms prompting further workup.
keyPoints: Identify clonal plasma cell/B lymphoproliferative process.
Renal: Proteinuria often in nephrotic range.
Cardiac: Low voltage ECG, diastolic dysfunction on echocardiogram.

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for AL Amyloidosis
items.content: In suspected cases of AL amyloidosis, serum protein electrophoresis and urine protein electrophoresis are not reliable screening tests due to often insufficient monoclonal 'M-spike' or Bence Jones proteins. Instead, immunofixation electrophoresis (SIFE/UIFE) or nephelometric measurement of free light chains should be used, as they detect monoclonal light chains in over 90% of patients.
keyPoints: Immunofixation electrophoresis and nephelometry are preferred for detecting monoclonal light chains in AL amyloidosis.
>90% detection rate with SIFE/UIFE or free light chain measurement.
Serum protein electrophoresis and urine protein electrophoresis often fail to detect sufficient monoclonal proteins.

items.title: Bone Marrow Examination in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: A bone marrow examination revealing an increased percentage of plasma cells, typically 5–30% of nucleated cells, is found in approximately 90% of patients with AL amyloidosis. Kappa or lambda clonality should be confirmed using flow cytometry, immunohistochemistry, or light chain mRNA in situ hybridization.
keyPoints: Increased plasma cell percentage (5–30%) in bone marrow common in ~90% of patients.
Kappa or lambda clonality confirmation is essential.
Use flow cytometry, immunohistochemistry, or light chain mRNA in situ hybridization for confirmation.

items.title: Staging and Risk Stratification of AL Amyloidosis
items.content: The Mayo 2004 staging system, modified by European investigators, uses NT-proBNP and cardiac troponins to identify high-risk patients. The Mayo 2012 modification includes clonal burden assessed by dFLC levels, which independently predicts survival. A renal staging system based on urine protein excretion and eGFR also exists.
keyPoints: Mayo 2004 staging uses NT-proBNP and cardiac troponins for risk stratification.
Mayo 2012 includes clonal burden (dFLC) to predict survival.
Renal staging based on urine protein excretion and eGFR predicts progression to dialysis.

items.title: Treatment Approaches and Outcomes in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: High-dose intravenous melphalan followed by autologous stem cell transplantation (HDM/SCT) is a primary treatment for AL amyloidosis, achieving complete hematologic responses in approximately 40% of patients. These responses are more durable compared to multiple myeloma, with some remissions lasting beyond 15 years without further treatment.
keyPoints: Complete hematologic response rate: ~40%
Remissions can last >15 years
Only ~20-30% of patients suitable for HDM/SCT due to higher morbidity and mortality rates

items.title: Alternative Therapies for SCT-Ineligible Patients
items.content: For patients ineligible for stem cell transplantation, CyBorD (cyclophosphamide, bortezomib, and dexamethasone) with daratumumab is recommended. Treatment regimens should consider patient characteristics, such as genetic markers like gain of 1q21 or t(11;14), which affect outcomes.
keyPoints: CyBorD plus daratumumab for SCT-ineligible patients
~15-20% are high-risk due to advanced cardiac stage or severe heart failure

items.title: Supportive Care and Management of Complications in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: Supportive care is crucial for managing symptoms such as nephrotic syndrome, amyloid cardiomyopathy, and autonomic neuropathy. Diuretics are used cautiously due to potential interactions with amyloid fibrils, and specific treatments like atrial ablation may be necessary for conduction abnormalities.
keyPoints: Diuretics for edema; caution with ACE inhibitors
Digitalis, calcium channel blockers, beta blockers relatively contraindicated in cardiomyopathy
Atrial ablation effective for atrial fibrillation

items.title: Etiology, Incidence, and Diagnostic Considerations of AA Amyloidosis
items.content: AA amyloidosis is associated with chronic inflammatory states or infections. Advances in therapy have reduced its incidence to fewer than 2% among patients with these conditions in the U.S. and Europe. It's crucial to consider CT scanning for tumors like Castleman's disease, lymphomas, and renal cell carcinoma when diagnosing AA amyloidosis.
keyPoints: Associated with chronic inflammatory states (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis) or infections (e.g., tuberculosis).
Incidence in the U.S. and Europe is fewer than 2% due to advances in therapy.
CT scanning important for diagnosing associated tumors like Castleman's disease, lymphomas, renal cell carcinoma.

items.title: Pathology and Primary Therapy of AA Amyloidosis
items.content: Kidneys are primarily affected in AA amyloidosis; other organs include the liver, spleen, and heart. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying inflammatory or infectious cause to reduce SAA levels and slow fibril formation. Colchicine is effective for familial Mediterranean fever but not other causes of AA amyloidosis.
keyPoints: Kidneys are primarily affected; hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, autonomic neuropathy, and cardiomyopathy can occur.
Cardiomyopathy occurs in ~10–25% of patients as a late manifestation.
Primary therapy involves treating the underlying disease to reduce SAA levels.
Colchicine is effective for familial Mediterranean fever but not other AA amyloidosis causes.

items.title: ATTRv Amyloidosis and V122I Variant in African-Americans
items.content: ATTR variant amyloidosis, particularly the V122I transthyretin mutation, is significant in African-American populations. This mutation is associated with late-onset cardiac amyloidosis, manifesting as concentric cardiac hypertrophy and diastolic heart failure without hypertension or valvular disease.
keyPoints: V122I TTR variant occurs in nearly 4% of African-Americans and Afro-Caribbean populations.
Consider V122I ATTR amyloidosis in African-American patients with concentric cardiac hypertrophy and diastolic heart failure, especially without hypertension or valvular disease.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.title: ATTRwt Amyloidosis: Prevalence and Clinical Features in Aging Men
items.content: Wild-type transthyretin (ATTRwt) amyloidosis is prevalent in aging men, particularly those over 80 years old. It manifests primarily as cardiac involvement but can also affect soft tissues, often preceding neuropathy or cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: ATTRwt amyloidosis occurs in 25% of hearts from male patients aged 80 and older at autopsy.
ATTRwt is the most prevalent form of amyloidosis globally due to aging populations.
Soft tissue involvement (e.g., tendon rupture) often precedes cardiac or neuropathic symptoms by one to two decades.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Hereditary Amyloidosis
items.content: Diagnosing hereditary amyloidosis involves screening for plasma cell disorders and identifying specific protein mutations, with DNA sequencing being the gold standard. Mass spectrometry can detect amino acid variations but is not definitive.
keyPoints: Screen for plasma cell disorders in patients with suspected amyloidosis.
DNA sequencing is essential for diagnosing specific hereditary amyloidosis mutations.
Mass spectrometry detects amino acid sequence variations but does not definitively identify protein mutations.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.title: Survival and Therapeutic Strategies in ATTR Amyloidosis
items.content: Untreated ATTR amyloidosis has a survival range of 4–15 years, varying by whether it primarily affects the heart or nervous system. Current therapeutic strategies include orthotopic liver transplantation (OLT) for specific cases, stabilization of TTR tetramers with small-molecule agents like diflunisal and tafamidis, and gene silencing using RNA interference agents such as patisiran and inotersen.
keyPoints: Survival after onset: 4–15 years depending on organ involvement
OLT is limited to early peripheral neuropathy (V30M ATTR) with minimal systemic amyloid burden
TTR stabilizers like diflunisal and tafamidis slow progression of polyneuropathy and cardiomyopathy
Gene silencers improve sensory nerve deficits in 35–60% of familial amyloid polyneuropathy patients

items.title: Emerging Treatments and Unmet Needs in ATTR Amyloidosis
items.content: Future clinical trials are exploring CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing and ATTR amyloid-depleting antibodies for ATTR polyneuropathy or cardiomyopathy. These antibodies target misfolded TTR epitopes, potentially disrupting amyloid deposits. Advances have extended survival but also revealed leptomeningeal and vitreous ATTR disease, underscoring the need for treatments that penetrate the blood-brain barrier.
keyPoints: CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing and amyloid-depleting antibodies are under investigation
Antibodies target misfolded TTR epitopes to disrupt amyloid deposits
Advances have highlighted leptomeningeal and vitreous ATTR disease, indicating a need for blood-brain barrier penetration

items.title: Recognizing Aβ2M Amyloidosis in Hemodialysis Patients
items.content: Aβ2M amyloidosis is a condition that occurs due to the accumulation of β2-microglobulin, which is not adequately cleared by dialysis membranes. It commonly presents with rheumatologic symptoms such as carpal tunnel syndrome and persistent joint effusions, particularly in patients undergoing long-term hemodialysis.
keyPoints: Aβ2M amyloidosis results from elevated β2-microglobulin levels due to kidney excretion issues.
The molecular mass of β2M is 11.8 kDa, which exceeds the cutoff for some dialysis membranes.
Carpal tunnel syndrome often presents as the first symptom.
Persistent joint effusions were found in up to 50% of patients on dialysis for more than 12 years.
Symptoms are bilateral and affect large joints like shoulders, knees, wrists, and hips.
The incidence is declining with newer high-flow dialysis membranes.

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Aβ2M Amyloidosis
items.content: Diagnosis of Aβ2M amyloidosis involves identifying β2-microglobulin deposits in affected tissues, often using Congo red staining. This condition should be considered in patients with unexplained symptoms like nephropathy or cardiomyopathy, especially those on long-term hemodialysis.
keyPoints: Congo red staining can identify β2M amyloid in synovial fluid and tissue biopsies.
Consider Aβ2M amyloidosis in patients with unexplained nephropathy or rheumatologic symptoms.
No specific therapies exist, but renal allografting may improve symptoms.
Diagnosis involves immunologic, biochemical, and genetic testing for accurate typing.

items.question: Which term was coined by Rudolf Virchow in 1854 to describe protein deposits that resemble starch under the microscope?
items.explanation: The term 'amyloid' was coined by Rudolf Virchow, who thought these deposits resembled starch.

items.question: What is the most prevalent form of familial amyloidosis?
items.explanation: ATTR amyloidosis, derived from transthyretin (TTR), is the most prevalent form of familial amyloidosis.

items.question: Which tissue is most easily accessible and positive in more than 80% of patients with systemic amyloidosis for biopsy?
items.explanation: Abdominal fat is the most easily accessible tissue positive in more than 80% of patients with systemic amyloidosis.

items.question: What unique property does amyloid exhibit when stained with Congo red dye and viewed under polarized light microscopy?
items.explanation: Amyloid exhibits a unique 'green' birefringence when stained with Congo red dye and viewed under polarized light microscopy.

items.question: According to the 'amyloid hypothesis,' what is considered the most toxic species in amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Accumulating evidence suggests that oligomeric intermediates may constitute the most toxic species.

items.question: Which cardiac biomarkers are elevated in amyloid cardiomyopathy and can be used for disease staging?
items.explanation: Amyloid cardiomyopathy is characterized by elevation of BNP or NT-proBNP as well as troponin.

items.question: What laboratory finding is frequently elevated in patients with amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Blood counts are usually normal, although the erythrocyte sedimentation rate is frequently elevated.

items.question: What is the most common type of systemic amyloidosis diagnosed in North America?
items.explanation: AL amyloidosis is the most common type of systemic amyloidosis diagnosed in North America.

items.question: Which organ is involved in 60–70% of AL amyloidosis patients?
items.explanation: The kidneys are a frequently involved organ and are affected in 60–70% of patients.

items.question: What is the leading cause of death from AL amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death from AL amyloidosis.

items.question: Which light chain subtype predominates in AL amyloid fibrils?
items.explanation: Although all kappa and lambda light chain subtypes have been identified, lambda subtypes predominate.

items.question: Which test is most sensitive for detecting low concentrations of monoclonal protein in AL amyloidosis?
items.explanation: More sensitive mass spectrometry-based assays can detect low concentrations of monoclonal protein.

items.question: What is the primary reason serum or urine electrophoresis may not be useful for screening AL amyloidosis?
options: The clonal light chain is often present in sufficient amounts.
Clonal light chain or whole immunoglobulin is often not present in sufficient amounts.
Electrophoresis cannot differentiate between AL and other types of amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Serum or urine electrophoresis may not be useful because the clonal light chain or whole immunoglobulin is often not present in sufficient amounts to produce a monoclonal M-spike.

items.question: Which method has become the diagnostic standard for accurate tissue amyloid typing?
options: Immunoelectron microscopy and tandem mass spectrometry-based typing
items.explanation: Immunoelectron microscopy and tandem mass spectrometry-based typing of the amyloid precursor protein have become the diagnostic standard.

items.question: What is a significant predictor of survival in AL amyloidosis according to the Mayo 2012 staging system?
options: Clonal burden assessed by dFLC concentration
items.explanation: The Mayo 2012 staging system includes clonal burden, assessed by dFLC concentration, which predicts survival.

items.question: What percentage of AL amyloidosis patients are suitable for aggressive treatment such as high-dose intravenous melphalan followed by autologous stem cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Only ~20-30% of all AL amyloidosis patients are suitable for aggressive treatment.

items.question: Which therapy is FDA-approved for transplant-ineligible AL amyloidosis patients?
items.explanation: The best therapy for transplant-ineligible patients is based on CyBorD (cyclophosphamide, bortezomib, and dexamethasone) with daratumumab.

items.question: What is a common complication in AL amyloidosis that increases mortality rates due to adsorption of clotting factor X?
items.explanation: A bleeding diathesis resulting from adsorption of clotting factor X to amyloid fibrils increases mortality rates.

items.question: Which medications are relatively contraindicated in treating congestive heart failure due to amyloid cardiomyopathy?
options: digitalis, calcium channel blockers, and beta blockers
items.explanation: Digitalis, calcium channel blockers, and beta blockers are relatively contraindicated as they can interact with amyloid fibrils.

items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT commonly associated with AA amyloidosis?
items.explanation: AA amyloidosis is associated with chronic inflammatory states like rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and ankylosing spondylitis, but not diabetes mellitus.

items.question: What percentage of patients with AA amyloidosis in the United States and Europe have no identifiable underlying disease?
items.explanation: In up to 20% of patients, AA amyloidosis can occur without any identifiable underlying disease.

items.question: Which therapy has failed in phase 3 trials for preventing or disrupting fibril formation in AA amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Development of eprodisate, a fibril-specific agent, failed in phase 3 trials for preventing or disrupting fibril formation.

items.question: Which TTR variant is associated with late-onset cardiac amyloidosis in nearly 4% of the African-American and Afro-Caribbean populations?
items.explanation: The V122I TTR variant is associated with late-onset cardiac amyloidosis in African-American and Afro-Caribbean populations.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.question: What is the most prevalent form of hereditary amyloidosis worldwide?
items.explanation: The most prevalent form of hereditary amyloidosis worldwide is ATTR variant (ATTRv) amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.question: What clinical feature commonly precedes nerve or heart manifestations in ATTRwt amyloid patients?
items.explanation: Soft tissue disease, including carpal tunnel syndrome, commonly precedes nerve or heart manifestations in ATTRwt amyloid patients.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.question: Which diagnostic method is the standard for identifying specific AF mutations?
items.explanation: DNA sequencing is the diagnostic standard for identifying specific AF mutations.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.question: Which therapeutic strategy is primarily used to treat ATTRv amyloidosis by replacing the source of the mutated protein?
items.explanation: OLT is used to replace the source of mutated protein in ATTRv.

items.question: What is a limitation of orthotopic liver transplantation (OLT) in treating patients with extensive amyloid burden?
options: It often leads to posttransplant disease progression.
items.explanation: OLT can lead to posttransplant disease progression in patients with extensive amyloid burden.

items.question: Which agents are used to stabilize circulating TTR tetramers, preventing the release and misfolding of monomeric protein?
items.explanation: Diflunisal and tafamidis stabilize circulating TTR tetramers.

items.question: Which of the following is a common initial symptom of Aβ2M amyloidosis in patients undergoing long-term hemodialysis?
items.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome is often the first symptom of Aβ2M amyloidosis.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of synovial fluid in patients with Aβ2M amyloidosis?
items.explanation: The synovial fluid is noninflammatory in Aβ2M amyloidosis.

items.question: Which of the following statements about the molecular mass of β2-microglobulin (β2M) is correct?
options: It is below the cutoff of some dialysis membranes.
It is 11.8 kDa, above the cutoff of some dialysis membranes.
It is 5.0 kDa, making it easily filtered by all dialysis membranes.
Its molecular mass varies significantly among patients.
items.explanation: The molecular mass of β2M is 11.8 kDa—above the cutoff of some dialysis membranes.

items.statement: The term amyloid was derived from the Greek word for protein.
items.explanation: The term 'amyloid' was coined by Rudolf Virchow, who thought these deposits resembled starch (Latin amylum).

items.statement: Mass spectrometry-based analysis is considered the best approach for identifying non-AL amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Mass spectrometry-based analysis has higher sensitivity and specificity than immunochemical techniques.

items.statement: Renal insufficiency can falsely elevate levels of cardiac biomarkers in amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Renal insufficiency can falsely elevate levels of BNP, NT-proBNP, and troponin.

items.statement: Amyloidosis typically causes significant alterations in aminotransferase levels.
items.explanation: Patients with liver involvement usually have minimal alteration of the aminotransferases.

items.statement: Macroglossia is a pathognomonic sign of AL amyloidosis seen in more than 50% of patients.
items.explanation: Macroglossia, a pathognomonic sign of AL amyloidosis, is seen in only ~10% of patients.

items.statement: The true incidence of AL amyloidosis may be much higher than the estimated 8–12 cases per 100,000 population.
items.explanation: Ascertainment continues to be inadequate, and the true incidence may be much higher.

items.statement: Monoclonal serum protein alone is diagnostic of amyloidosis.
items.explanation: A monoclonal serum protein by itself is not diagnostic of amyloidosis due to the commonality of monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain significance.

items.statement: Patients with smoldering myeloma should be screened for AL amyloidosis if they have signs or symptoms of renal, cardiac, or neurologic disease.
items.explanation: Screening for AL amyloidosis is recommended in patients with smoldering myeloma who exhibit relevant organ involvement.

items.statement: Hematologic responses after HDM/SCT in AL amyloidosis patients are less durable compared to those in multiple myeloma.
items.explanation: Hematologic responses appear to be more durable after HDM/SCT than in multiple myeloma.

items.statement: Patients with localized AL amyloidosis generally require systemic treatment.
items.explanation: Localized AL amyloidosis may respond to surgical intervention or low-dose radiation therapy; systemic treatment is generally not appropriate.

items.statement: Cardiomyopathy is an early manifestation of AA amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Cardiomyopathy is a late manifestation in ~10–25% of patients with AA amyloidosis.

items.statement: Colchicine is the standard treatment for familial Mediterranean fever and effective for all causes of AA amyloidosis.
items.explanation: While colchicine is the standard treatment for familial Mediterranean fever, it has not been helpful for AA amyloidosis of other causes.

items.statement: Normal transthyretin can misfold and aggregate to form ATTR amyloid, primarily affecting men in their seventh decade.
items.explanation: Wild-type transthyretin misfolding leads to ATTRwt amyloidosis, mainly expressed in older men.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.statement: The incidence of familial amyloidoses is estimated at over 1/100,000 population in the United States.
items.explanation: Familial amyloidoses have an estimated case incidence of less than 1/100,000 population in the U.S.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.statement: TTR gene silencers have been shown to halt the progression of neurologic disease in patients with familial amyloid polyneuropathy.
items.explanation: TTR gene silencers reliably halt neurologic disease progression.

items.statement: Orthotopic liver transplantation (OLT) is the most commonly used treatment for ATTR cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: OLT is rarely employed now, limited to specific cases of ATTRv.

items.statement: Visceral deposits of Aβ2M amyloid are common in patients with this condition.
items.explanation: Visceral β2M amyloid deposits occur rarely.

items.statement: Renal allografting may lead to symptomatic improvement in Aβ2M amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Cessation of dialysis after renal allografting can improve symptoms.

items.assertion: AL amyloidosis is associated with clonal B-cell or plasma cell disorders.
items.reason: This disorder arises from immunoglobulin light chains, which are related to these cell types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: AA amyloid is composed of transthyretin.
items.reason: AA amyloid occurs in chronic inflammatory or infectious diseases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; AA amyloid is composed of serum amyloid A, not transthyretin.

items.assertion: Renal amyloidosis in AL amyloidosis usually manifests as proteinuria.
items.reason: Proteinuria is often in the nephrotic range and associated with hypoalbuminemia, secondary hypercholesterolemia, and hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the manifestation of renal amyloidosis.

items.assertion: The presence of a multisystemic illness or general fatigue should prompt a workup for amyloidosis.
items.reason: These symptoms are nonspecific and often not considered until specific organ-related symptoms develop.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these symptoms should prompt further investigation.

items.assertion: Immunoelectron microscopy is more reliable than immunohistochemical staining for amyloid typing.
items.reason: Commercial antibodies used for immunohistochemistry may not be accurate in amyloid typing.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immunoelectron microscopy is preferred.

items.assertion: The Mayo 2004 staging system for AL amyloidosis includes dFLC concentration.
items.reason: dFLC concentration was added in the Mayo 2012 modification to predict survival.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as dFLC was not part of the Mayo 2004 system; it was included in the 2012 update.

items.assertion: High-risk patients with advanced cardiac stage (IIIb) are often excluded from clinical trials.
items.reason: These patients have severe heart failure, which poses significant risks during treatment trials.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: Amyloid cardiomyopathy can be effectively treated with beta blockers.
items.reason: Beta blockers interact with amyloid fibrils and may worsen heart failure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because beta blockers are relatively contraindicated; the reason is true.

items.assertion: Treatment that suppresses or eliminates the inflammatory state can decrease circulating levels of SAA.
items.reason: Suppressing inflammation reduces hepatic production of SAA, slowing amyloid fibril formation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why suppressing inflammation decreases SAA levels.

items.assertion: Incomplete penetrance is a reason for apparent sporadic presentations of ATTRv amyloidosis.
items.reason: Sporadic presentations often reflect incomplete penetrance rather than spontaneous events.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES

items.assertion: ATTR amyloid involvement of the heart is clinically worse tolerated than AL amyloid cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: The time from heart failure presentation to death in untreated cases of ATTR is shorter than that for AL amyloidosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ATTR amyloid involvement of the heart is better tolerated than AL amyloid cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Amyloidosis · ATTR AND OTHER HEREDITARY AMYLOIDOSES
